author: plzgimmeA
title: Chill guy, the new prof is nice :D
comment_content: same as previous semesters, you can check the previous reviews for the exact content.
comment_teaching:
This is the first time this professor has taught this course (I think this is his first semester in HKUST).

Most of the lecture materials were kept the same as previous semesters (made by Prof. Gibson Lam and David Rossiter, I think Prof. Rossiter left UST this semester). BUT THE MAIN DIFFERENCE IS THAT THE PROFESSOR ADDED LOTS OF CAT MEMES INTO THE LECTURE SLIDES (I LOVE CATS AUUUGHHHHHHH).

Anyway, jokes aside, the professor really explained everything thoroughly and used sufficient examples to back up my understanding of different concepts. In general, his teaching style is "beginner-friendly" in a way that helps you solidify what's written in the lecture notes, so that even if you're new to all these HTML and JS stuff you can still understand everything pretty easily. Also, he responds to your email, but the TAs do not.
comment_grading:
4 Hand-in Labs (30%) [most ppl get full marks for all labs (not me)]
lab 1: not required to submit
lab 2: 7%
lab 3: 7%
lab 4: 7%
lab 5+6: 9%
(you have to build on top of lab 5's code to hand in lab 6)
Total: 7%+7%+7%+9%=30%

1 Group Project (30%) [LQ 280/Median 290/UQ 300/Full 300]
You have to make a multiplayer game for the group project. Make sure you find someone that you can really work with (preferably group with them to take this course), or it may be tough for you to do the project.

1 Final Exam (40%) [LQ 58/Median 71.5/UQ 83/Full 100]
Open notes exam. You can print out all lecture notes and bring them into the exam.

Typical UST (A$$) grading. Below UQ and u get a solid B range even if u get full for all other assignments. Could've done better but still :< (it sucks to fall just below UQ)
comment_workload:
Acceptable imo since you dont have mid-term or tutorials.

Each lab probably only takes you a few hours to finish. Definitely doable by spending a weekend afternoon to finish.

Project, welp, just make sure you fulfill all requirements from the project grading scheme in the pdf.

BUT YO WHAT THE HELL THE EXAM IS CRAZY. BRO NEED US TO READ THROUGH 2x PAGES OF HTML/JS CODE DURING EXAM. WHAT THE HELLLLLL. Some exam questions are tricky, especially when you're tired of reading all dat 💩 and suddenly some tricky questions pop out. Make sure you stay mentally strong and lock in during the exam. (lol)

Hope this helps :)

author: Kabul
title: Nice course.
comment_content:
HTML, CSS, JavaScript, jQuery, SVG, XML, AJAX, PHP, Bootstrap, JSON, Cookies, Node.js, everything you can name related to web development. The content is very hands-on and practical, and I'm glad that I finally have a COMP course that teaches me how to code...

The course is beginner-friendly and no prerequisite knowledge in web programming is needed. Dave has amended the course topics to remove technologies that are obsolete and include those that are more recent and commonly used. It still does not cover the more advanced technologies such as React, AngularJS and Vue.js, but this is fair enough given the limited time for the course. If you wanna be a full stack developer you probably also need to learn Linux, Apache/Nginx, depending on which stack you would like to specialize in. But I guess you'd need to explore them by yourselves.
comment_teaching:
The COVID-19 pandemic has made us switch to online learning this semester. However, so far I think Dave has done a very good job in delivering course materials and helping us consolidate what we've learned. Dave made "high-efficiency" videos that are clear, concise and suitable for lazy guys like me. He also provided a Zoom session every week and asked a few basic questions to help us revise what we've just learned. Some questions involved tricky points we easily miss out and asking questions is indeed a very nice way to bring them out.

PS It was so funny that Dave forgot to escape the characters in his chatroom code example and somebody else in class easily did an XSS hacking during his demo LMAO. Then a message box kept hovering over the web page.
comment_grading:
💻 30% - 3 Hand-in Labs (Each 10%)
✏️ 30% - Late Midterm
💻 40% - Project (8% Proposal, 32% Project Deliverable)

Extra 0.6% for every question you answer right in the Zoom review sessions, and he asked 48 questions in total. So a maximum of 28.8% bonus points could be added on top of your overall score. That's a hell lot of points!

The midterm was a little online Canvas quiz consisting of 15 multiple choice questions, randomly drawn from a super-set of question database. Everyone would get a slightly different subset of questions. It was conducted quite late towards the end of the Spring semester, in May, so it actually covered most of the lecture materials for the whole semester. Open-book and open Internet. But only Chrome was allowed and of course no messaging. The midterm was easy but tricky, with the mean landing on only 61.7/100. We've got some interesting questions though, like one asking us to go to a web page and check the expiry date of a cookie, and another one asking us to send a HEAD HTTP request and check the last modified date of an HTML file. This would only be possible when we have an online midterm like this. But on the other hand Dave also liked to ask "eye test" questions such as spotting a missing speech mark, which I personally think are silly since we now have powerful IDEs that can easily fix all these.

Mean = 61.7/100
SD = 17.5
Max = 92.4
Min = 14.2

As for the project, 8% of the total score is allocated to the project proposal. As soon as you've written something intelligible, you should be able to get it. The remaining 32% is for the actual project deliverable. You can either do great in a project with only client-side programming, or do okay-ish in both the client-side and server-side programming to get full score.

Mean = 26.6 / 32 (Does not include 0's)
SD = 8.4

I got 86.67/100 in the midterm, 22.8/28.8 for bonus Zoom questions, 28/32 for the project (got some bugs ☹️), and full marks in the lab assignments.

Final grade: Pass
So I dunno what I actually got. 🤷‍♂️
comment_workload:
The workload mainly lies on the lab assignments and the course project.

There were a total of 5 labs, but only lab 2 to lab 4 needed to be submitted. Since it's online instruction they've basically become assignments with strictly defined marking schemes.

🔷 Lab 1
A number guessing game introducing basic CSS, JavaScript and DOM handling.
🔶 Lab 2
A hammer game involving jQuery and the use of timer.
🔶 Lab 3
A obstacle jumping game involving SVG and animations.
🔶 Lab 4
A movie catalog allowing users to search and sort Marvel movies from an XML database. Mainly involved PHP, Bootstrap and XML handling.
🔷 Lab 5
A single-page application allowing users to view, filter, sort and add Pokémon. Mainly involved PHP, Bootstrap, jQuery, XML and AJAX.

🔷 Hand-in Lab
🔶 Self-Learning Lab

Most of the labs were easy and could be finished in a couple of hours. Mean score were crazily high though, so make sure don't lose any easy marks here.

Everything else is simple. but all evil lies in the course project! We can either do it alone or form a group of 2 - 3 people. The topic can either be an extension from the lab (probably can only score an okay-ish mark if we do that) or any other fancy topics we like. At the end we've gotta submit to link to the main page of our website and make a video to introduce what it does / make an appointment to demonstrate to Dave real-time. The difficulty actually depends on the selection of the topic and the group mates. It's still very challenging, however, since the project is indeed assessed in terms of complexity and most of the time the content taught in class is just not enough for a complex project. Specifically the server-side programming such as PHP and Node.js is only taught in brief and there is a lot we have to explore by ourselves if we wanna do a project that includes server-side programming.

But overall the workload is lighter than most of the other COMP courses.

author: Kpyau
comment_content:
Use of HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Jquery, SVG, PHP

Three assignments: 12%(jQuery)+18%(SVG)+18%(PHP)=48%
One Midterm: 18%
One Final 34%
comment_teaching: Kenneth is a good teacher. It is easy for us to follow as he speaks English fluently. Each lesson is short. If he finished teaching the slides, we can go early. Has a review session for us to practice some exam question.
comment_grading:
Good grade
Almost full marks in three assignments
mid term and exam scores are around mean
Got B+
comment_workload:
Workload is low
Make sure you attend the lab because the TA will guide you do the lab which is related to the assignments
If you attend all the labs, the assignments are done like 70%

author: sheepy
title: Depends on your groupmate
comment_content:
See others for the detail
-basic HTML,CSS syntax
-JS frontend
-JS backend (yeah i get so confused during this part)
-animation using requestAnimationFrame and recursion

For me who dont know any JS before, this course is great as it talks about backend too.
A bit too much for a final but overall ok for learning.
comment_teaching:
Great.
idk what to say but his teaching is good.
Like the older review, really loves how the cat memes are embedded in ppt and they really helped me concentrate and understand the contents.
comment_grading:
A few simple labs, a project and a final.
Not much to say as the grading scheme is clearly written in the website and the project introduction ppt.
Only a few marks are deducted in our project (maybe the game is not fun enough?)

The final is quite funny as it requires us to develop different frontend and backend systems for a IVE concert XD. Some answers like HTTP code 500 is hidden somewhere in the notes but at least it is open notes so it's ok I guess. There are questions on JSON,CSS,JS, etc. but mostly short and direct. The questions here are wayyyyyyy easier than 3021 anyway.
comment_workload:
Not much workload for the labs as you can use Copilot.

For the final, it is open-notes but it would be better if you memorize some syntax like promise or CSS to JS codes.

For the project, we started only a week before the deadline so we rushed till the end. I really want to thank my groupmates (as we never meet in reality) for your gaming codes. The login page is easy but making a real-time synchronizing game is really exhausting. The workload mainly comes from the project so better start early.

author: B.T.
title: Professor a bit overrated. A good course overall.
comment_content:
Refer to the 2022 Spring Review for Gibson for the detailed breakdown of lecture materials.

One point that I dislike about the contents, apart from being quite out-dated, is that the topics are not inter-related from class to class. It sometimes jumps to JS, then jumps back to more on CSS, then more on JS, then HTML forms, then backend, then suddenly more on frontend... A bit hard to follow the logic. But if you can clear up your mind and understand everything, it should be fine with the ireegular order of topics.

Also, this course is perhaps one of the few decentralized course in HKUST. Different professors will introduce way different topics. Therefore, suggest you look into other reviews to comapre the detailed contents that each teaches if you think the topics will be one of your concern for enrollment and if you can waitlist into this course.
comment_teaching:
Firslty, showing my background, I have taken various "famous" professors in CSE, such as Dave Rossiter, Desmond, Kenneth, DY Leung, etc. This is the first time (and last, ofc.) taking Gibson's class. Maybe I expected too much, or maybe I thought his teaching would be identical to Rossiter's, anyhow, I don't feel his lecture enjoyable. I will say he is an average instructor.

The professor did have his heart to teach, and prepare pretty well for each class. However, I fell asleep in almost every lecture. Probably due to the fact that lecture time is TuTh 5-6pm, and in LT-K where the cusions are very nice. The professor tried to make jokes from time to time but I don't think them particularly funny.

Also, this is a bit personal, but I just don't like Gibson's intonation. His accent is fine, pronunciation is claer, but his way of presenting linkages between sentences make me hard to concentrate and to digest what every single word that he speaks.

After the add-drop, there are about only 10 students in LT-K where the enrollment size is in full capacity 80. This also shows that Gibson may not be as enjoyable as we expect.

Even though, he delivers the content and maintains the logistics well. One extra comment is that the TA graded the lab and project really, really slow. It took nearly two months to grade the first lab.
comment_grading:
Labs 3 x 10% (4 choose 3 best)
Project 30%
Final Exam 40%

The four labs are hammer game, jumping game, gem game and chatroom. Project is to develop a web game with real-time connection between two players. (Yes..... I don't know why the professor is so obsessed with games. Anyway, they are fine)

Labs are easy. Follow the lab videos and check with the grading scheme to score full.

Project is tricky, in the sense that you may think everyone is super good, doing cool games. Indeed, it's not the case. Follow the marking scheme and do every single element and then you will be already top 10%. This is not a game programming course at the end of the day.

Final exam: A comp1021 style of final exam, tricky but easy questions. Open book open notes closed internet and closed programming. However, I don't know what's the point of testing us syntax-related stuff and some for-loops or some functions in javascript. Or maybe there is no other things to test at all? Idk.

My grade:
Labs: Full 30% (means are higher than 95% for each lab, and considering choose 3 out of 4, mean for total lab should around full)
Project: 240 out of 250 (mean 230)
Final Exam: 93 (mean 68.4)

Letter grade: A+
Very good indeed. I was expecting an A only.
comment_workload:
Not bad. Labs have videos released where you can just follow all and write 2-3 lines to get full score.

The last lab may need to spent more time but is fine. The project is the most heavy part where you need to spend days and nights with your groupmates to work on it.

One key tip: Actually not many groups make wonderful games even Gibson showed many samples at the beginning. Follow all the grading criteria and secure most of the scores before making some cool stuff. Fyr, my game is just an extension of a lab because we did't have enough time to make it nice, yet it fulfills all grading criteria and just lose 10 out of 250 points on "gaming experience".

author: SOSYBOI
title: Nice professor. flexible and fun.
comment_content:
For this part, it is pretty much the same as previous semesters. You learn from client side to server side starting from html, css , js all the way to nodejs, php. For details, please look at other reviews.

The only thing I will say is Javascript is the worst programming language of all time. It is definitely useful for me. But I absolutely hate this language so much after working on the project and the code.
comment_teaching:
Really nice professor. The way he taught was engaging, sometimes having some funny memes on the slide and showing some fun examples to make things easier to understand. I think it is very welcoming to absolute beginners.

He is also willing to answer your questions. Even if he can't answer them immediately, he will usually respond relatively quick on email or put the answer in the announcement. Genuinely a very nice professor!

I haven't contacted with the TAs except one of them being kinda harsh on our project since he not only did not email/inform me but my other groupmate (He didn't told me anything about it). He couldn't start our server from the project and he didn't give any meaningful feedbacks but also being aggressive on us saying "you are violating the rules of the project" since he cannot open it. We have tried to help but it didn't work. Again, shout out to Wentao for handling the problem.
comment_grading:
This is not absolute graded. Most likely I will not get A or above but I think it is really not that hard to get a really good grade.

There are 6 labs with 4 hand in labs.
The first one is not required. And lab 5 and 6 are combined into one hand in lab.
The total weighs 7% + 7% + 7% + 9% = 30%

I think it is really easy to get full marks on this. I will explain this on the workload session.

Project grading is also 30%. Most got full marks. I got 265/300. If you care, you can look at the last paragraph of me yapping on why I didn't get full in workload session. We made a game of players trying to mine as much minerals as possible and whoever gets higher score wins. The score is based on the rarity of the minerals and the amount. If you are interested, let me know in the comments and I will post the files here.

Exam: 40%. I think it is easy to get 70+ marks since it is open book and most of them are similar to the labs. The only issue is that the exam question can be very long and sometimes confusing. But really easy to get very high score. I got 84/100.

I got a B+ in the end. Honestly, it is quite expected. Probably like 15-20 people have A range. others just B range or below.
comment_workload:
Overall, pretty flexible. If I didn't take this course, I probably will get a really really hard time for this semester. I had so much trouble for other courses.

For labs, you get a lot of time finishing each and one of them. Some of the labs deadline are in 2 weeks or even 3-4 weeks.

There is not much you need to do for studying the exams. You can practice on VSCode on this. I think that is not going to do much.

The project workload really is based on how you and your groupmate can do. And make sure to start it early. If you have all that, then it should be easy to get full marks or higher score than us.

My group mate and I were busy and I was in a situation where I have to finish 7 HWs and 1 lab in two weeks and I did not even start working on the project. The worst part is, I did most of the work by myself and I have to work on the project without sleep during that week and the time for the project was supposed to be pretty flexible! I did the pixel art, most of the coding. My groupmate was not very helpful either. He didn't know how to use github or how to add features properly in the project and I had to spend a lot of time to figure out what he did while figuring out how to do everything on my side. I HAVE TO DO WHAT HE WAS SUPPOSED TO WORK ON! The only thing he contribute the most is the video recording. I couldn't believe I was able to finish the project honestly. Also, Javascript made me spent so much time to debug since it will never tell you what is the issue. Not even console.log can save you! Never trust anyone who says "Javascript is an elegant language".

author: Matthew YU
title: Chill professor but not chill course
comment_content: Same lecture notes and labs each semester . Contents are barely updated. Prof will add some cute memes to lecture notes to make fun, but that’s all.
comment_teaching: Teaching is great. But since it’s comp course you can imagine only a few people come to class. This may be hard for you to find a group mate and also hard for prof to make class interactive and vibrant.
comment_grading: Grading is normal. The main differences are project and final. Due to keen competition this semester over 90% overall score cannot secure A range. And remember to do paper check since TAs are very likely to wrongly calculate the score for your final. I don’t know why.
comment_workload: Workload is manageable. Make sure you have someone that can really work with. Projects are really competitive this semester. Even If you want to get an average marks, you should design a really good and creative game. Final is not that difficult.

author: A0
comment_content:
HTML, CSS, JavaScript, jQuery, AJAX, SVG, PHP
This course only teaches the very basic in internet computing. (Especially for summer course)
It is very outdated and does not teach the more advanced ways of creating a website. (Such as node.js, angular, bootstrap, React, Angular).

But, if you choose to take this course in the regular semester, they do teach more things such as bootstrap, responsive web designs, angular js, restful api. (After all, they have more time to teach).
comment_teaching:
Kennith is a great teacher.
The TA is probably the best TA ever. He would stay behind after lab (14:00) until the deadline of SVG game (24:00) to help the students who are deadline fighting. So nice :)
comment_grading:
Kennith gives out really good grades :)
Statistics:
The mean and s.d. of midterm is about 77 and 14.3.
The mean and s.d. of final is about 69.something and 11.something.

Really good grade.
comment_workload:
In the summer, need to finish 3 assignments, which means that you have only a week or so to finish each assignments.

The first one is the autocomplete assignments. It is easy, but if you don't rely on sources and is unfamiliar with modifying or extending the jQuery UI's widgets, you might need to take some time to research the implementation for the assignments.

The second one is SVG game. This assignment's work load is quite high as there are a lot of requirements for the game, and debugging would take a while. Really recommend not to dead line fight. Do it as early as possible. But, doing this assignment is quite fun and enjoyable.

The third assignment is super easy. Just need to change some minor things from the labs and you're done.

Have mid term and final, but is quite easy.
Oh and also, the midterm and final are open notes, open laptop exams, but cannot connect to the internet. :)

author: Leo Chan
comment_content:
The content is really outdated. It won't have much practical use in the workplace. Might have to relearn things.
You get a lot of practice on Javascript and SVG. Not so much for jQuery, AJAX and PHP, as a result of them providing most of the code for the related assignments.
comment_teaching: He is easy to listen to. But you would learn better by reading the powerpoints alone rather that listening to him. He hardly explaining anything in detail. As a result, he often dismisses his class early, which is nice. That said, he is willing to answer questions
comment_grading: It is probably quite good for a COMP course. I am about the 33.3th percentile and got a B+. It is not hard to get A range .
comment_workload: Content-wise there's not much to this course as the exams are open-book exams. The assignments are quite troublesome and many ended up not being able to complete them. However he simplified it a lot for the summer sem and most of it can be copied from the labs.

author: elessar
title: falling behind the tide
comment_content: The content of the course falls so much behinds the tide.   The first few classes are about basic HTML, CSS,JAVASCIPT, one class for Jquery and then 4 classes for SVM. This is bbefore the midterm. After the midterm comes PHP and some theoretical stuff. The course takes too much emphasis on SVM and PHP, while it should be focusing on new languages instead.
comment_teaching: his teaching is quite well, to be honest. With everything quite clear. Nevertheless, the clearness is based on the knowledge of the listeners. If we knew the content, then everything he says makes sense. Otherwise his teaching is all messed up.
comment_grading: Objectively speaking, the grading is very nice. With mean at B+ I believe. Because of a contempt for the content of the course, I skipped most of the lectures and the labs, failed to accomplish 2 assignments, almost became the last on the list of course ranking, and got a B.
comment_workload: The workload is OK. Following the  lab will help save much time. 3 Projects are ther in total. One Jquery, one SVM, one PHP.

comment_workload: Two exams are open-book open-notes, so we wouldn't have to memorize the syntax and function calls. Three projects are easy and require not much time. You can absolutely find source online :).

author: Moritz
title: Easy and interesting overview of internet technologies
comment_content:
It's a quick tour through most popular internet technologies. Starting with HTML, CSS and JavaScript going over to XML and PHP. For most topics it's just scratching the surface.

I liked the fact that jQuery was incuded as well, since it is very popular these days and allows for some cool results with few lines of code.

The XML part I found to be a little bit too detailed compared to the rest.
comment_teaching: Teaching in general is very good. However I found that the materials are sometimes are little old and optically not very consistent. There's quite a lot of old and deprecated stuff in the Lab code as well as on the slides. A revision for JavaScript ES6 and PHP7 would be appropriate.
comment_grading: Exams are pretty easy, but that also means that everybody does very well and it is rather hard to get a good grade.
comment_workload: Workload depends very much on how much effort you put into the lab assignments. When you copy & paste everything from Stack Overflow it's not too bad. When you really want to put work into it, it can get very exhaustive.

author: AP2112
title: New Prof, Good Prof

comment_content:

Basics for web development, includes front-end and back-end. Pretty useful with a lot of hands-on opportunities, rare for your average COMP course in UST.
comment_teaching:
Online lectures only. 2-3 Videos posted every week. Powerpoints are very detailed so watching the videos isn't really necessary to be honest.

However, since the lecture is covered by Gibson and Professor Rossiter, the videos (when you do need them) are very well made, examples are explained in even MORE detail, and you can watch them at your own pace. Very good!
comment_grading:
Labs (30%): 4 labs, only the 3 highest ones are counted, the first 3 labs are significantly easier than the 4th, expect most people to have 100% on all 3.

Course Project (30%): Group project to create your own multiplayer game, with some specifications like a live view of the opponents gameplay, account system and results screen. Quite useful and fun since you are doing something hands-on, grades are given on both functionality and gameplay. (Average: 202/250)

Final (40%): The only part of this course that isn't good. The final is a 95 minute long, COMP1021 style exam, it is open note however, so just print out everything. Questions range from extremely easy, to just annoying to do since you have to read multiple pages of code to fill in ONE blank, or read 2 pages of a .JSON file to spot ONE missing bracket, what were they thinking? (Average: 66.12/100)

I got 9% over mean, and still only a B+, disappointing.
comment_workload:
Not bad.

The first 3 labs are quite easy, and can be finished in an hour or two. The last lab is harder since it is more complex, and is split into two parts, while it isn't counted in the grade if you get full marks on the first 3 labs, it's still worth doing in my opinion.

The exam is pretty easy to prepare, go through the notes and print them out. Nothing too difficult, wish the course just skipped the exam, and counted the project instead.

Speaking of which, the project, like all projects, depends on how good your groupmates are, so there is always some risk involved. I got lucky and had great groupmates, so the workload was good (since I don't have to fix their parts).

title: One of the BEST comp course in ust
comment_content:

comment_teaching:
Dave is fun and speak some standard(?) English, which is very pleasant to sit in his lecture.
His teaching is great, keep showing us examples with great explanation.
This course teaches you as like you never do internet computing. So don't be scared if you forgot how to do html.
Since there are quite a lot of contents are included in the course, it does go quite fast and you have to pick up some concept in your own time.
Dave also wanted to encourage us to go to lecture, so sometimes he asked us questions, and when we got it right, we can add an extra 0.6% into our percentage. So do pay attention! But no worries if you get it wrong.

And for the TAs, Sophia and Young, they are both helpful and they guide you through the lab exercises during the lab section. But to me,  they went through the lab a bit too fast therefore I did easily got lost.
comment_grading:
3 graded lab (9% each) - 27%
(you can't choose which 3, they stated it inside the lab when you do it)
a project (project itself 20%, propose your idea 4%) - 24%
(you can do it on your own or a maximum of two groupmate)
(most people did this on their own in this sem)
midterm - 22%
final - 27%

I got a B- with a percentage of around 77.84%, mean is around 79.753%.
comment_workload:
This course is quite packed so you have to pick up some concept in your own time.
Labs are quite hard in my opinion, but TAs will help you but still, stick with lecture notes and W3SCHOOL.
You need to propose your own project, but just handle your part as a big lab exercise. I did it on my own and mine is quite sh__ but I got a full 24%.
And exams, they are hard. Although they are open books, we don't have much time to flip and find answers. Questions are short, and do really test your understanding. For example, mc ask you about the output of code, gives you four pieces of code and ask which is the only one that works... etc etc

author: ppowoe
title: Easy 4xxx course, mini project is not mini
comment_content:
Separated into 2 half.

First half is frontend. Things like HTML, CSS, JavaScript and jQuery is taught, and they talk about concepts like module pattern and promise. The beginning is pretty easy if you learn them in other course before. The part about promise may take some time to understand.

Second half is backend. Talk about things about HTTP, cookies, session, web socket, RESTful API, MVC, and how to use Node.js to create server, etc.


Labs (30%)
Total 6 labs, 4 submitted labs, lab 5 and 6 is working on the same thing but you do it in 2 weeks and finish different part of the lab.

Lab 1 Guessing game (not submitted)
Lab 2 Hammer game
Lab 3 Jumping game
Lab 4 Gem Rush
Lab 5/6 Chatroom

Mini project (30%)
Create your own multiplayer game, or extend from an lab to make it multiplayer. Work in a team of 2 or 3 people. Need to prepare a 1 to 2 page project description and a short demo video (around 3 minutes)

Final exam (40%)
The scope is everything taught in the course. Open book exam. Calculator allowed.
comment_teaching:
Online learning. You just watch videos uploaded to the course website anytime you like. Sometimes David is teaching and sometimes Gibson. The teaching quality is just the same as you attend David's or Gibson's class face to face.

I think the audio quality can improve. You may not hear the voice clearly in some video clips, especially when you play it in 2x speed.

Max: 100

I very surprised that I can have 100 mark, despite some tricky questions in the exam. I've never thought of getting 100 in an exam of UST.

Final grade: A+, thanks to the final exam.
comment_workload:
Labs are easy and can be finished in few hours. The final exam is not hard also.

Workload mainly comes from the mini project (it is not mini to be honest). It will take you about several weeks to make a good game. Better to start working on it once you have found your teammate. Do not wait until few days before the deadline, otherwise you will have a hard time.
